title: VMware Aria Operations Command Injection CISA KEV: Unauthenticated RCE During Support Migration Enables Full Infrastructure Takeover Across All Aria Operations 8.x and Cloud Foundation 9.x Deployments (CVE-2026-22719, CVSS 8.1)

sources.name: CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog - CVE-2026-22719 Addition
Broadcom KB430349 - Workaround Instructions for CVE-2026-22719
BleepingComputer - CISA Flags VMware Aria Operations RCE Flaw as Exploited in Attacks
The Hacker News - CISA Adds Actively Exploited VMware Aria Operations Flaw to KEV Catalog
SecurityWeek - VMware Aria Operations Vulnerability Could Allow Remote Code Execution
SecurityOnline - Critical VMware Aria Operations Flaw Allows RCE During System Upgrades
CSO Online - VMware Fixes Command Injection Flaw in Aria Operations
GB Hackers - VMware Aria Operations Flaws Enable RCE and Privilege Escalation

timeline.event: Broadcom publishes VMSA-2026-0001 security advisory disclosing CVE-2026-22719 and releases patches for Aria Operations 8.18.6 and Cloud Foundation 9.0.2.0
Broadcom publishes KB430349 with workaround script (aria-ops-rce-workaround.sh) for organizations unable to immediately patch
Active exploitation confirmed in the wild by threat actors targeting VMware management infrastructure
CISA adds CVE-2026-22719 to Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog and mandates Federal Civilian Executive Branch (FCEB) agencies patch by March 24, 2026
Exploitation continues actively; Intelleo briefing published

simple_summary.what_happened: A dangerous security flaw was found in VMware Aria Operations, which is a popular tool used by IT teams in large companies to monitor and manage all their virtual computers and servers from one central dashboard. The bug allows attackers to inject malicious commands during a routine support migration process, gaining the ability to run any code they want on the server without needing a password or any login credentials.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Organizations running VMware Aria Operations version 8.x (all versions before 8.18.6) or VMware Cloud Foundation Operations 9.x (before 9.0.2.0). This includes enterprises, government agencies, and cloud service providers who use VMware's virtualization management platform. Federal agencies have been given a mandatory patch deadline of March 24, 2026.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attacker scans the internet for publicly exposed VMware Aria Operations management interfaces. Step 2: Attacker sends a specially crafted HTTP request to the migration service endpoint, embedding shell command separators (semicolons, pipes, backticks) in a parameter that is passed to the operating system. Step 3: The migration service processes the request without sanitizing the input, causing the system to execute the attacker's injected command with the service account's privileges. Step 4: Attacker establishes a reverse shell or deploys a backdoor, gaining persistent access to the Aria Operations server. Step 5: With control of the monitoring platform, the attacker harvests credentials for all managed VMs, moves laterally across the virtual infrastructure, and can shut down, encrypt, or exfiltrate data from any monitored system.
what_you_should_do: Immediately upgrade VMware Aria Operations to version 8.18.6 (for 8.x deployments) or Cloud Foundation Operations to 9.0.2.0 (for 9.x deployments)
If patching cannot be done immediately, download and execute the aria-ops-rce-workaround.sh script from Broadcom KB430349 on each Aria Operations node
Restrict network access to the Aria Operations management interface to trusted internal IP ranges only and block external access via firewall rules
Review Aria Operations logs for any unusual command execution, shell metacharacters in migration logs, or unexpected outbound network connections originating from the server
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: VMware Aria Operations sits at the center of enterprise virtual infrastructure — it has credentials and management access to every virtual machine in the organization. Compromising it gives attackers a master key to the entire virtualized environment, enabling ransomware deployment, mass data exfiltration, or complete infrastructure shutdown. CISA classified this as a Known Exploited Vulnerability, meaning attackers are already actively using it in real attacks right now.

tactics.description: Attacker exploits the unauthenticated command injection vulnerability in the Aria Operations migration service endpoint, gaining initial foothold without credentials
Injected shell metacharacters cause the migration service to execute arbitrary OS commands via the system shell, enabling code execution with service account privileges
Attackers deploy web shells or backdoors on the Aria Operations server to maintain persistent access after initial exploitation
With code execution on the monitoring platform, attackers can extract credentials for all managed vCenter and ESXi systems stored in the Aria Operations database
Using harvested credentials from Aria Operations, attackers pivot to managed virtual machines and infrastructure components across the entire VMware environment

detection_guidance.summary: Detection focuses on identifying anomalous command execution patterns in Aria Operations migration logs, unexpected processes spawned by the service account, and suspicious outbound network connections from the Aria Operations appliance.

log_sources.description: Migration service logs located at /var/log/vmware/vcops/ — look for entries containing shell metacharacters (;, |, &, `) in parameter values during migration operations
Monitor process creation events (execve syscalls) spawned by the Aria Operations service account (typically 'vcops' or 'admin') for unexpected shell commands
Alert on any outbound connections from the Aria Operations appliance to external IP addresses, particularly on non-standard ports (reverse shell indicators)
Correlate Aria Operations access logs with migration event timestamps to detect exploitation attempts during migration windows

what_to_look_for.indicator: Shell metacharacters in HTTP request parameters to migration endpoints
what_to_look_for.description: Requests containing ;, |, &, backtick, $(), or ${} in parameter fields destined for the migration API indicate injection attempts

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unexpected child processes spawned by Aria Operations service
what_to_look_for.description: The migration service should not spawn bash, sh, curl, wget, or other shell utilities as child processes during normal operation

what_to_look_for.indicator: Outbound network connections from Aria Operations appliance
what_to_look_for.description: Any outbound TCP connections from the Aria Operations appliance to external IPs not matching known Broadcom/VMware telemetry endpoints

what_to_look_for.indicator: New files created in /tmp or system directories by service account
what_to_look_for.description: Backdoors and reverse shells are commonly written to /tmp directories; monitor for new executable files created by the vcops service account
what_to_look_for.log_field: file.path starts_with '/tmp' AND process.user='vcops' AND file.type='executable'

immediate_actions.action: Apply VMware Aria Operations Patch Immediately
immediate_actions.description: Upgrade to Aria Operations 8.18.6 (for 8.x) or Cloud Foundation Operations 9.0.2.0 (for 9.x). Download patches from Broadcom Support Portal using advisory VMSA-2026-0001. Federal agencies must complete patching by March 24, 2026.

immediate_actions.action: Apply Temporary Workaround If Patching Is Delayed
immediate_actions.description: Download the aria-ops-rce-workaround.sh script from Broadcom KB430349 and execute it as root on each Aria Operations Virtual Appliance node. This mitigates the vulnerability without requiring a full upgrade.

immediate_actions.action: Restrict Network Access to Aria Operations Management Interface
immediate_actions.description: Immediately apply firewall rules to limit access to the Aria Operations management UI (typically port 443) to trusted internal IP ranges only. Block all external/internet access to this interface.
immediate_actions.command: iptables -I INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -s <trusted_range> -j ACCEPT && iptables -I INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j DROP

immediate_actions.action: Review Logs for Evidence of Exploitation
immediate_actions.description: Examine Aria Operations migration logs and system audit logs for shell metacharacters in request parameters, unexpected process execution, or new files created in /tmp directories. Treat any findings as a potential active breach.

immediate_actions.action: Rotate Credentials Stored in Aria Operations
immediate_actions.description: If exploitation cannot be ruled out, assume all credentials stored in Aria Operations (vCenter, ESXi, cloud accounts) have been compromised. Rotate passwords for all managed systems as a precaution.
